466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\) apply the Mathlib collapsing lemma
491	  \texttt{IsIntegral.exists\_multiple\_integral\_of\_isLocalization} (with
492	  \(R = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,A\), \(R_m = \mathrm{MvPoly}\,K\), \(S = B_K\), and the localizing
493	  submonoid the image of \(A^{0}\)): this returns a single
494	  \(a_x \in A^{0}\) (non-zero, as \(A\) is a domain) such that \(C(a_x)\cdot(1\otimes x)\)
495	  is integral over \(\mathrm{MvPoly}\,A = A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
496	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
497	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
498	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
499	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
500	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
501	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the landed proof discharges a whole generator
502	  at once via the collapsing lemma, which folds that primitive over the coefficients
503	  internally.)
504	
505	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
506	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
507	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
508	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
509	
510	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
511	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
512	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
513	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
514	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
515	  \[
516	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
517	  \]
518	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
519	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
520	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
521	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
522	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
523	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
524	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
525	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
526	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
527	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
528	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
529	
530	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
531	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
532	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
533	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
534	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
535	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
536	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
537	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
538	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
539	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
540	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
541	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
542	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
543	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
544	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
545	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
546	
547	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
548	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
549	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
550	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
551	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
552	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
553	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
554	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
555	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
556	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
558	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
560	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
561	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
562	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
563	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
564	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
565	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
566	\end{proof}
567	
568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
569	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
570	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
572	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
573	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
574	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
575	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
576	\end{lemma}
577	\begin{proof}
578	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
579	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
580	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
581	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
582	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
583	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
584	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
585	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
586	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
587	\end{proof}
588	
589	\begin{lemma}
590	
591	
592	\leanok
593	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
594	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
596	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
597	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
598	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
599	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
600	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
601	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
602	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
603	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
604	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
605	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
606	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
607	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
608	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
609	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
610	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
611	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
612	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
613	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
614	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
615	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
616	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
617	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
618	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
619	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
620	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
621	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
622	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
623	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
624	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
625	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
628	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
629	  \]
630	
631	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
632	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
633	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
634	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
635	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
636	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
637	\end{lemma}
638	
639	\begin{proof}
640	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
642	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
643	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
644	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
645	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
646	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
647	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
648	
649	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
650	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
651	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
652	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
653	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
654	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
655	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
656	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
657	
658	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
659	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
660	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
661	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
662	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
663	\end{proof}

675	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
676	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
677	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
678	  \mathlibok
679	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
680	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
681	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
682	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
683	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
684	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
685	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
686	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
687	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
688	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
689	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
690	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
691	\end{lemma}

693	\begin{lemma}\leanok
694	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
695	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
697	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
698	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
699	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
700	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
701	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
702	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
703	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
704	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
705	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
706	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
707	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
708	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
709	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
710	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
711	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
712	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
713	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
714	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
716	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
717	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
718	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
719	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
720	\end{lemma}
721	
722	\begin{proof}
723	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
724	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
725	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
726	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
727	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
728	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
729	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
730	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
731	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
732	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
733	\end{proof}

735	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
736	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
737	
738	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
739	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
740	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
741	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
742	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
743	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
744	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
745	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
746	
747	\begin{definition}\leanok
748	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
749	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
751	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
752	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
753	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
754	  \[
755	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
756	  \]
757	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
758	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
759	\end{definition}
760	
761	\begin{lemma}\leanok
762	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
763	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
764	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
765	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
766	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
767	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
768	\end{lemma}
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
771	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
772	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{definition}\leanok
776	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
777	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
779	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
780	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
781	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
782	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
783	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
784	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\begin{lemma}\leanok
788	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
789	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
791	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
792	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
793	\end{lemma}
794	\begin{proof}
795	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
796	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}\leanok
800	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
801	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
803	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
804	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
805	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
806	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
807	\end{lemma}
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
810	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
811	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
812	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
813	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
814	\end{proof}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}\leanok
817	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
818	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
820	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
821	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
822	  transformed monomial is the radix value
823	  \[
824	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
825	  \]
826	\end{lemma}
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
829	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
830	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
831	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
832	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
833	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}\leanok
837	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
838	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
840	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
841	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
842	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
843	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
844	\end{lemma}
845	\begin{proof}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
847	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
848	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
849	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
850	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
851	\end{proof}

853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
855	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
857	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
858	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
859	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
860	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
861	\end{lemma}
862	\begin{proof}
863	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
864	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
865	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
866	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
867	\end{proof}

869	\begin{lemma}\leanok
870	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
871	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
873	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
874	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
875	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
876	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
877	\end{lemma}
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
880	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
881	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
882	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
883	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
884	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
885	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
886	\end{proof}

944	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
945	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
946	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
947	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
948	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
949	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
950	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).

953	\begin{proof}
954	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
955	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
956	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
957	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
958	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
959	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
960	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
961	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
962	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
963	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
964	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
965	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
966	\end{proof}

1093	\begin{proof}
1094	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1095	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1096	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1097	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1098	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1099	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1100	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1101	
1102	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1103	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1106	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1107	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1108	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1109	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1110	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1111	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1112	
1113	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1114	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1115	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1116	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1117	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1118	  \(A_g\).
1119	
1120	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1121	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1122	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1123	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1124	
1125	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1126	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1127	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1128	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1129	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1130	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1131	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1132	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1133	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1134	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1135	
1136	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1137	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1138	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1139	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1140	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1141	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1142	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1143	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1144	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1145	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1146	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1147	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1148	
1149	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1150	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1151	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1152	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1153	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1154	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1155	
1156	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1157	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1158	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1159	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1160	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1161	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1162	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1163	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1164	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1165	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1166	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1167	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1168	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1169	
1170	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1171	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1172	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1173	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1174	\end{proof}
1175	
1176	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1177	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1178	
1179	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1180	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1181	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1182	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1183	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1184	
1185	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1186	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1187	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1191	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1192	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1193	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1194	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1195	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1196	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1197	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1198	\end{lemma}
1199	\begin{proof}
1200	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1201	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1202	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1203	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1204	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1205	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1206	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1207	\end{proof}
1208	
1209	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1210	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1211	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1213	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1214	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1215	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1216	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1217	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1221	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1222	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1223	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1224	\end{proof}
1225	
1226	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1227	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1228	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1230	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1231	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1232	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1233	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1234	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1235	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1236	\end{lemma}
1237	\begin{proof}
1238	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1239	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1240	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1241	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1242	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1243	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1244	  these conditions gives the claim.
1245	\end{proof}
1246	
1247	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1248	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1249	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1250	  \mathlibok
1251	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1252	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1253	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1254	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1255	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1256	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1257	\end{lemma}
1258	
1259	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1260	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1261	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1262	  \mathlibok
1263	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1264	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1265	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1266	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1267	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1268	\end{lemma}

1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1272	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1274	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1275	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1276	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1277	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1278	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1279	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1280	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1281	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1282	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1283	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1284	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1285	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1286	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1287	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1288	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1289	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1290	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1291	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1292	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1293	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1294	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1295	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1296	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1297	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1298	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1299	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1300	\end{lemma}
1301	
1302	\begin{proof}
1303	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1304	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1305	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1306	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1307	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1308	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1309	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1310	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1311	
1312	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1313	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1314	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1315	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1316	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1317	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1318	
1319	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1320	  \[
1321	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1322	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1323	  \]
1324	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1325	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1326	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1327	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1328	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1329	
1330	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1331	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1332	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1333	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1334	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1335	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1336	\end{proof}

1338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1339	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1340	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1342	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1343	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a

1345	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1346	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1347	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1348	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1349	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1350	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1351	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1352	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1353	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1354	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1355	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1356	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1357	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1358	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1359	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1360	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1361	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1362	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1363	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1364	  core of the induction).}
1365	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1366	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1367	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1368

1371	\begin{proof}
1372	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1373	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1374	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1375	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1376	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1377	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1378	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1379	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1380	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1381	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1382	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1383	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1384	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1385	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1386	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1387	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1388	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1389	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1390	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1391	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1392	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1393	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1394	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1395	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1396	
1397	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1398	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1399	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1400	
1401	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1402	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1403	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1404	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1405	  \begin{enumerate}
1406	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1407	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1408	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1409	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1410	      \(P_d\)-modules
1411	      \[
1412	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1413	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1414	      \]
1415	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1416	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1417	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1418	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1419	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1420	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1421	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1422	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1423	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1424	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1425	      to \(A_g\).
1426	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1427	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1428	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1429	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1430	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1431	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1432	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1433	      over \(A_f\).
1434	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1435	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1436	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1437	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1438	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1439	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1440	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1441	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1442	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1443	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1444	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1445	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1446	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1447	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1448	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1449	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1450	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1451	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1452	      completes the induction.
1453	  \end{enumerate}
1454	\end{proof}
1455	
1456	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1457	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1458	
1459	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1460	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1461	
1462	\begin{lemma}
1463	
1464	
1465	
1466	\leanok
1467	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1468	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1470	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1471	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1472	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1473	\end{lemma}
1474	\begin{proof}
1475	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1476	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1477	\end{proof}
1478	
1479	\begin{lemma}
1480	
1481	
1482	
1483	\leanok
1484	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1485	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1486	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1488	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1489	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1490	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1491	  is free over \(A_f\).
1492	\end{lemma}
1493	\begin{proof}
1494	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1495	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1496	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1497	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1498	\end{proof}
1499	
1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1514	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1516	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1517	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1518	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1519	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1520	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1521	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1522	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1523	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1524	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1525	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1526	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1527	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1528	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1529	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1530	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1531	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1532	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1533	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1534	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1535	\end{lemma}
1536	
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1539	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1540	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1541	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1542	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1543	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1544	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1545	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1546	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1547	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1548	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1549	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1550	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1551	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1552	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1553	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1554	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1555	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1556	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1557	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1558	\end{proof}
1559	
1560	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1561	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1563	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1564	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1565	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1566	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1567	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1568	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1569	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1570	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1571	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1572	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1573	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1574	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1575	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1576	\end{lemma}
1577	
1578	\begin{proof}
1579	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1580	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1581	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1582	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1583	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1584	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1585	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1586	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1587	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1588	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1589	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1590	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1591	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1592	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1593	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1594	\end{proof}
1595	
1596	
1597	\begin{theorem}
1598	
1599	
1600	
1601	\leanok
1602	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1603	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1605	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1606	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1607	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1608	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1609	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1610	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1611	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1612	  %
1613	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1614	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1615	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1616	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1617	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1618	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1619	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1620	  %
1621	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (matches the landed .lean file):
1622	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1623	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] [QuasiCompact p] (F : X.Modules)
1624	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1625	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1626	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1627	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1628	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1629	  %
1630	  % NOTE (iter-023): `[QuasiCompact p]` is essential — LocallyOfFiniteType does NOT
1631	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1632	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1633	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1634	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1635	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
1636	
1637	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1638	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1639	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1640	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1641	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1642	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1643	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1644	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1645	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1646	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1647	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1648	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1649	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1650	
1651	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1652	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1653	\end{theorem}
1654	
1655	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1656	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1658	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1659	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1660	% by restricting to a non-empty
1661	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1662	\begin{proof}
1663	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1664	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1665	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1666	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1667	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1668	
1669	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1670	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1671	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1672	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1673	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1674	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1675	
1676	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1677	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1678	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1679	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1680	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1681	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1684	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1685	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1686	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1687	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1688	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1691	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1692	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1693	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1694	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1695	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1696	\end{proof}

1698	\section{Mathlib status}
1699	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1700	
1701	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1702	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1703	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1704	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1705	generic-flatness argument consumes
1706	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1707	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1708	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1709	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1710	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1711	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1712	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1713	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1714	are introduced in
1715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
