1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
284	\end{lemma}
285	
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
338	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
340	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
341	  restriction map
342	  \[
343	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
344	  \]
345	  read as a map of \(R'\)-modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) as the
346	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). This is
347	  the \(R'\)-side localization fact transported in movement~(3) of the proof of
348	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
349	\end{lemma}
350	
351	\begin{proof}
352	
353	
354	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
355	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
356	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
357	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
358	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
359	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
360	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
361	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
362	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
363	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
364	\end{proof}
365	
366	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
367	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
368	on their proof sketches.
369	
370	\begin{lemma}
371	
372	\leanok
373	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
374	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
376	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
377	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
378	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
379	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
380	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
381	  itself.
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	
386	
387	
388	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
389	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
390	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
391	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
392	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
393	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
394	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
395	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
396	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
398	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
399	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
400	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
401	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
402	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the
403	  basic-open computation below.
404	\end{proof}
405	
406	\begin{lemma}
407	
408	\leanok
409	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
410	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
411	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
412	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
413	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
414	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
415	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
416	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
417	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
418	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	
423	
424	
425	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
426	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
427	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
428	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is
429	  the structure-sheaf restriction. Both \(j\) and \(\rho\) exhibit their targets as
430	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N,\top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) — \(j\) by the
431	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
432	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)
433	  with \(e \circ j = \rho\); the triangle identity then forces \(L = e\), so \(L\) is
434	  an isomorphism. (This is the per-basic-open input feeding the basis-local
435	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
436	\end{proof}
437	
438	\begin{lemma}
439	
440	\leanok
441	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
442	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
444	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
445	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
446	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
447	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
448	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
449	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
450	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
451	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
452	  \[
453	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
454	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
455	  \]
456	\end{lemma}
457	
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
460	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
461	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
462	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
463	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
464	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion
465	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} upgrades this to: the counit
466	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) is an isomorphism, i.e.\ \(N\) lies in the
467	  essential image of \(\widetilde{(-)}\). Composing the inverse counit with the
468	  tilde of the global-sections comparison
469	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} yields the asserted object isomorphism.
470	\end{proof}
471	
472	\begin{lemma}\leanok
473	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
474	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
476	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
477	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
478	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
479	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
480	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
481	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
482	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
483	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
484	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
485	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
486	% is hand-proved per a.
487	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
488	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
489	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
490	  \[
491	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
492	    \;\cong\;
493	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
494	  \]
495	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
496	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
497	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
498	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
499	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
500	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
501	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
502	
503	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
504	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
505	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
506	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
507	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
508	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
509	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
510	\end{lemma}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
514	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
515	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
516	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
517	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
518	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
519	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
520	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
521	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
522	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
523	
524	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
526	  \[
527	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
528	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
529	  \]
530	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
531	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
532	
533	  \medskip
534	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
535	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
536	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
537	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
541	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
542	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
543	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
544	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
545	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
546	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
547	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
548	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
549	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
550	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
551	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
552	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
553	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
554	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
555	  an isomorphism.
556	
557	  \medskip
558	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
559	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
560	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
561	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
562	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
563	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
564	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
565	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
566	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
567	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
569	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
570	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
571	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
572	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
573	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
574	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
575	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
576	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
578	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
580	  movements.
581	
582	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
583	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
584	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
585	  \[
586	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
587	      \;\cong\;
588	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
589	  \]
590	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
591	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
592	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
593	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
594	
595	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
596	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
597	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
599	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
601	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
602	  \[
603	    \begin{array}{ccc}
604	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
605	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
606	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
607	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
608	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
609	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
610	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
611	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
612	    \end{array}
613	  \]
614	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
615	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
616	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
617	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
618	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
619	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
620	  \(\widetilde M\).
621	
622	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
623	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
624	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
625	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
626	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
627	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
629	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
630	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
631	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
632	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
633	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
634	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
635	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
636	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
637	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
638	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
639	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
640	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
641	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
642	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
643	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
644	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
645	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
646	
647	  \medskip
648	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
649	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
650	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
651	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
652	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
653	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
654	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
655	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
656	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
657	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
658	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
659	  naturality square for the inclusion
660	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
661	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
662	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
663	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
664	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
665	
666	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
667	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
668	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
669	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
670	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
671	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
672	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
673	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
674	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
675	
676	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
677	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
678	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
679	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
680	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
681	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
682	  % bump.
683	  \medskip
684	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
685	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
686	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
687	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
688	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
689	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
690	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
691	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
692	  needed for the affine lane.
693	\end{proof}
694	
695	\begin{remark}
696	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
697	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
698	  \begin{enumerate}
699	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
700	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
701	      \(R\)-module of sections of
702	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
703	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
704	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
705	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
706	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
707	      closed under isomorphism; hence
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
709	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
710	      needed for the affine lane.
711	  \end{enumerate}
712	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
713	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
714	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
715	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
716	\end{remark}
717	
718	\subsection{The pullback companion}
719	
720	The affine reduction below requires, alongside the pushforward dictionary, the
721	\emph{pullback} companion: the affine description of the inverse image of a
722	tilde-module. It is the affine case of the classical fact that the pullback of a
723	quasi-coherent sheaf is computed on modules by base change of scalars.
724	
725	The pullback companion is built by uniqueness of left adjoints from the
726	per-object \(\Gamma\)-fragment comparison of
727	Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, packaged as a natural isomorphism of
728	right-adjoint functors (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}).
729	
730	\begin{lemma}
731	
732	\leanok
733	[Naturality packaging of the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison]
734	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardNatIso}
736	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
737	  The per-object comparison of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} assembles into a
738	  natural isomorphism of the two right-adjoint functors
739	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)}\) and
740	  \(\widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) on modules, exhibiting the
741	  pushforward dictionary as a natural transformation rather than merely a family of
742	  object isomorphisms. This is the input to the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints argument
743	  that produces the pullback companion Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}.
744	\end{lemma}
745	\begin{proof}
746	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
747	  The components are the object isomorphisms of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso};
748	  naturality is the corresponding naturality square, which holds because every
749	  constituent of the comparison is identity-on-elements.
750	\end{proof}
751	
752	\begin{lemma}
753	
754	\leanok
755	[Affine pullback of a tilde-module]
756	  \label{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
757	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
758	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}
759	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma `lemma-widetilde-pullback', Tag 01I9
760	  % (\S\,Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
761	  % L1241--1256).
762	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let
763	  % $(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = (\Spec(S), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(S)})$,
764	  % $(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) = (\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$
765	  % be affine schemes.
766	  % Let $\psi : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
767	  % morphism of affine schemes, corresponding to the ring map
768	  % $\psi^\sharp : R \to S$ (see Lemma \ref{lemma-category-affine-schemes}).
769	  % \begin{enumerate}
770	  % \item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
771	  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$.
772	  % \item We have $\psi_* \widetilde N = \widetilde{N_R}$ functorially
773	  % in the $S$-module $N$.
774	  % \end{enumerate}"
775	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Lemma ``\(\widetilde M\) and pullback''
776	  (Tag 01I9), part (1).}
777	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
778	  be an \(R\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
779	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-modules
780	  \[
781	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\,\widetilde{M}
782	    \;\cong\;
783	    \widetilde{\bigl(R' \otimes_R M\bigr)},
784	  \]
785	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
786	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
787	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
788	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
789	  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
790	  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
791	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
792	  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
793	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)
794	  of the same Stacks lemma of which the pushforward formula is part~(2).
795	\end{lemma}
796	
797	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The first assertion follows from the identification in
798	% Lemma \ref{lemma-compare-constructions}
799	% and the result of Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-restrict-quasi-coherent}."
800	\begin{proof}
801	  \uses{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
802	  Pullback along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, on quasi-coherent sheaves, the
803	  extension-of-scalars operation \(- \otimes_R R'\) on modules transported through
804	  the \(\widetilde{(-)}\) / \(\operatorname{moduleSpec}\Gamma\) equivalence between
805	  modules and quasi-coherent sheaves. Concretely, the global sections of
806	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M}\) are the base change
807	  \(R' \otimes_R M\): the pullback functor \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{*}\) is
808	  left adjoint to the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\), and under
809	  the global-sections identification the latter is restriction of scalars along
810	  \(\varphi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}); the left adjoint of
811	  restriction of scalars is extension of scalars \(M \mapsto R' \otimes_R M\). The
812	  comparison map is the unit of the base-change adjunction, and the universal
813	  property of base change identifies it with the canonical isomorphism
814	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)^{*}\widetilde{M} \cong
815	  \widetilde{(R' \otimes_R M)}\). Naturality in \(M\) is the naturality of that
816	  unit. (One may equally read this off the standard-open description: on
817	  \(D(\varphi a)\) the localization \((R' \otimes_R M)[1/a]\) is
818	  \(R'[1/\varphi a] \otimes_{R[1/a]} M[1/a]\), which is the base change of the
819	  sections of \(\widetilde M\) over \(D(a)\).)
820	\end{proof}

822	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
823	
824	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
825	reduces, via the locality criterion
826	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
827	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
828	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
829	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
830	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
831	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
832	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
833	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
834	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
835	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
836	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
837	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
838	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
839	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
840	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
841	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	
845	\leanok
846	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
847	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
849	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
850	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
851	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
852	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
853	  % L920--926).
854	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
855	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
856	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
857	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
858	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
859	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
860	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
861	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
862	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
863	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
864	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
865	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
866	  by the affine-open locality criterion
867	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
868	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
869	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
870	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
871	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
872	
873	\end{lemma}
874	
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
877	  By the affine-open locality criterion
878	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
879	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
880	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
881	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
882	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
883	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
884	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
885	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
886	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
887	
888	  \medskip
889	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
890	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
891	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
892	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
893	  \[
894	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
895	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
896	  \]
897	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
898	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
899	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
900	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
901	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
902	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
903	
904	  \medskip
905	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
906	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
907	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
908	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
909	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
910	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
911	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
912	  \begin{itemize}
913	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
914	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
915	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
916	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
917	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
918	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
919	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
920	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
921	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
922	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
923	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
924	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
925	  \end{itemize}
926	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of

928	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
929	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
930	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open

936	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
937	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no

954	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
955	
956	The section-level identification
957	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
958	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, a concrete \(R'\)-linear map
961	\(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the content of the lemma is
962	that this map is the inverse of the cancellation isomorphism
963	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Because the base-change map is \emph{defined} as the
964	transpose of the inner pushforward comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under the
965	\((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)), its section-level value
966	is read off by the \emph{conjugate-counit calculus}: unfolding the transpose through the
967	counit form of the hom-set adjunction
968	(\(\operatorname{Adjunction.homEquiv\_counit}\)) factors \(\theta\) as
969	\(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\), and
970	conjugating this composite by the two tilde dictionaries
971	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and
972	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) replaces the
973	geometric counit by the \emph{algebraic} extension-of-scalars counit along \(\psi\). This
974	is the route the live formalization takes: the cancellation identity
975	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} routes through the generator
976	trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace}, which is a one-line corollary of the
977	section identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}, whose single residual
978	obligation is the counit-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}.
979	The per-generator/element evaluation is \emph{not} available as a shortcut: evaluating the
980	opaque geometric composite on \(1 \otimes m\) produces no element-level normal form that
981	\(\operatorname{rfl}\) or \(\operatorname{simp}\) can reduce, so the abstract conjugate
982	calculus is the only tractable discharge. Two Mathlib results are recorded
983	first as dependency anchors.
984	
985	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a pair of affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps]
986	  \label{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}
987	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
988	  \mathlibok
989	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
990	  Let \(R \to S\) and \(R \to T\) be \(R\)-algebras. Then the fibre product of the
991	  induced morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(S) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) and
992	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(T) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is canonically the spectrum of
993	  the tensor product,
994	  \[
995	    \operatorname{Limits.pullback}\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}(R\to S),\,
996	      \operatorname{Spec}(R\to T)\bigr)
997	    \;\cong\; \operatorname{Spec}\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr)
998	    \qquad (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}),
999	  \]
1000	  and under this isomorphism the two cone legs are the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of
1001	  the canonical tensor inclusions: \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_fst} identifies the
1002	  first projection with \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
1003	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom} : S \to S \otimes_R T\), and
1004	  \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_snd} identifies the second projection with
1005	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
1006	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight} : T \to S \otimes_R T\) (with companion
1007	  forms \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_inv\_fst}, \texttt{\_inv\_snd}).
1008	\end{lemma}

1249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1250	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1251	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1253	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1254	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1255	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1256	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1257	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	\begin{proof}
1260	
1261	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1262	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1263	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1264	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1265	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1266	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1267	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1268	\end{proof}

1276	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1277	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1278	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).

1284	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.

1291	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1292	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1293	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1295	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1296	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1297	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1298	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1299	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1300	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1301	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1302	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1303	  % L933--938).
1304	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1305	  % equality
1306	  % $$
1307	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1308	  % $$
1309	  % as $R'$-modules."
1310	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1311	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1312	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1313	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1314	  \[
1315	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1316	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1317	  \]
1318	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1319	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1320	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1321	  hypothesis.
1322	\end{lemma}
1323	
1324	\begin{proof}
1325	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1326	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1327	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1328	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1329	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1330	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1331	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1332	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1333	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1334	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1335	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1336	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1337	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1338	
1339	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1340	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1341	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1342	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1343	  dictionaries,
1344	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1345	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1346	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1347	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1348	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1349	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1350	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1351	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1352	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1353	  \[
1354	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1355	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1356	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1357	  \]
1358	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1359	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1360	
1361	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1362	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1363	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1364	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1365	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1366	\end{proof}

1374	\begin{lemma}
1375	
1376	\leanok
1377	[The affine pullback--pushforward unit is the algebraic unit]
1378	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_unit_value}
1380	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1381	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma `lemma-affine-base-change'
1382	  % (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the "boils down to the equality" step
1383	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L932--937).
1384	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the equality
1385	  % $$ (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M $$ as $R'$-modules."
1386	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
1387	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1388	  Let \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) be the canonical inclusion
1389	  (\(a \mapsto a \otimes 1\)). The unit of the
1390	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\)-adjunction
1391	  evaluated at the affine sheaf \(\widetilde M\), read on the global sections over
1392	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) through the tilde dictionaries
1393	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and
1394	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) unit \(\widetilde{(-)}\Gamma\)-iso, equals the algebraic unit of the
1395	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\iota_A\), namely
1396	  \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1397	\end{lemma}
1398	\begin{proof}
1399	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1400	  This is the affine, square-free heart of the computation: there is no pullback square and
1401	  no base-change map yet, only the comparison of two units of two adjunctions related by the
1402	  two tilde dictionaries. The geometric adjunction
1403	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^* \dashv (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\) on
1404	  sheaves of modules transports, under the tilde dictionaries which identify
1405	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*\) of a tilde with extension of scalars
1406	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\) of a tilde
1407	  with restriction of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}), to the algebraic
1408	  extension/restriction adjunction \((\iota_A^{\,*} \dashv \iota_{A\,*})\) on
1409	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these
1410	  adjunctions, naturality identifies the two units; evaluating at \(M\) gives the stated
1411	  value \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1412	\end{proof}

1647	  identity upgrades to a full isomorphism equality
1648	  \[
1649	    \mathrm{pullbackComp}_{f,g}
1650	      \;=\; \operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g}),
1651	  \]
1652	  so the conjugate re-encoding may rewrite \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\) for
1653	  \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}\) (and back) freely in the comparison object.
1654	  \begin{proof}
1655	    \uses{lem:pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv}
1656	    An isomorphism in a category is determined by its inverse: if two isomorphisms have equal inverses
1657	    they are equal, and an equality of isomorphisms is detected on the underlying morphisms. Applying
1658	    these to the inverse-level identity of
1659	    Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_inv_eq_leftAdjointCompIso_inv} gives the stated equation.
1660	  \end{proof}
1661	\end{lemma}

1663	\begin{lemma}
1664	
1665	\leanok
1666	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1667	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}

1669	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1670	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1671	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1672	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1673	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1674	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1675	  composition coherences. Then
1676	  \[
1677	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1678	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1679	    \;=\;

1681	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1682	  \]

1695	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1696	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1697	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by

1937	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1938	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
1939	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
1940	  \mathlibok
1941	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1942	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
1943	  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
1944	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
1945	  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
1946	  \[
1947	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
1948	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
1949	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

77	% against it.
