79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  The structure morphism is the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); it is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$

241	  \]
242	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
245	\end{theorem}

284	\begin{proof}
285	  
286	  \leanok
287	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
288	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
289	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
290	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
291	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +
292	  % ``relativeGluingData.toBase_preimage_eq_opensRange_ι`` +
293	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
294	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
295	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
296	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
297	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
298	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
299	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
300	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
301	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
302	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
303	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
304	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
305	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
306	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
307	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
308	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
309	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
310	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
311	\end{proof}

313	\section{Affine base}
314	\label{sec:relspec_affine}
315	
316	\begin{theorem}\leanok
317	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
318	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
320	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
321	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
322	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
323	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
324	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
325	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
326	  % is available.
327	  
328	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
329	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
330	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
331	  % "In Situation REF.
332	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
333	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
334	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
335	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
336	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
337	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
338	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
339	  \[
340	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
341	  \]
342	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
343	  spectrum of the global sections.
344	\end{theorem}
345	
346	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
347	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
348	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map
349	% \(\)
350	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
351	% \longrightarrow
352	% S = \Spec(R).
353	% \(\)
354	% We have
355	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
356	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
357	% Hence there is a canonical map
358	% \(\)
359	% \varphi_{univ} :
360	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
361	% \longrightarrow
362	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
363	% \(\)
364	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
365	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
366	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."
367	\begin{proof}
368	  
369	  \leanok
370	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
371	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
372	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
373	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
374	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than
375	  % the canonical iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))'' named below (iter-174+
376	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
377	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
378	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
379	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
380	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
381	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
382	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
383	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
384	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
385	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
386	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
387	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
388	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
389	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
390	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
391	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
392	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
393	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
394	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
395	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
396	\end{proof}
397	
398	\section{Lean encoding}
399	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
400	
401	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
402	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
403	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
404	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
405	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
406	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
407	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
408	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
409	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
410	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
411	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
412	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
413	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
414	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
415	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
416	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
417	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
418	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
419	infrastructure.
420	
421	\section{Out of scope}
422	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
423	
424	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
425	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
426	are explicitly out of scope here:
427	\begin{itemize}
428	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
429	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
430	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
431	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
432	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
433	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
434	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
435	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
436	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
437	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
438	\end{itemize}

2400	  \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), and inside the opaque codomain

2406	  whole four-factor chain — for \emph{generic} morphisms \(g', f'\) carrying the cone-leg
2407	  equalities \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and
2408	  \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\) as explicit hypotheses, in place of the literal
2409	  projections \(\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2\). In this abstract form the legs are
2410	  free variables, not terms pinned by the pullback's universal property, so each can be
2411	  eliminated by substituting its defining equation: the equality proof \(w\), the adjunction
2412	  index, and the \(\gamma_{\psi}\)/\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) arguments are all expressed in the
2413	  free leg variables and transform uniformly under the substitution. This restatement converts
2414	  the blocked rewrite into a legitimate one; it is the structural heart of the step.
2415	
2416	  \emph{(ii) \(\Gamma\)-collapse of the transparent coherences.} On global sections, read through
2417	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2418	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2419	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2420	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2421	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2422	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2423	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2424	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2425	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2426	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2427	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2428	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2429	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2430	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2431	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2432	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2433	  sections.

2441	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit followed by \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\) of the

2446	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
2447	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2448	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2449	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
2450	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
2451	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely

2455	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)'' bookkeeping of the source. Each of (i)--(iii) extracts as a

2559	\begin{lemma}
2560	
2561	\leanok
2562	[(A-2a) The $e$-unit is an isomorphism]
2563	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}
2564	  % NOTE: superseded by the conjugate-side re-encoding (Open Q2). The e-unit invertibility it records
2565	  % is now consumed inside the conjugate-component identity of `_legs`, not as a standalone eCancel
2566	  % atom. Lean pin retained.
2567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}
2568	  \uses{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
2569	  Let \(e : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(N\) a \(Y\)-module. Then the unit
2570	  \(\eta^{e}_{N} : N \to e_{*}\,e^{*}N\) of the \((e^{*} \dashv e_{*})\)-adjunction is an
2571	  isomorphism. This is the single fact enabling cancellation~(1): in the inner telescoping it is
2572	  the \(e\)-unit factor \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\eta^{e}\) (here \(e\) is the canonical
2573	  square isomorphism \(\mathrm{pullbackSpecIso}\)), which the codomain read
2574	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) bakes in as its inverse \(\eta^{e}\)-piece, so the two cancel.
2575	\end{lemma}
2576	\begin{proof}
2577	  \uses{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
2578	  Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor \(e^{*} = \mathrm{pullback}\,e\)
2579	  is an equivalence (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}). The unit of an adjunction whose
2580	  left adjoint is an equivalence is an isomorphism; hence \(\eta^{e}_{N}\) is invertible.
2581	\end{proof}

2884	  to the explicit-composite form Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}, where the leg is
2885	  identified with its composite definitionally, removes the obstruction; the reindex coherence is then
2886	  discharged there on the conjugate side (Open Q2).
2887	\end{proof}
2888	
2889	\begin{lemma}
2890	
2891	\leanok
2892	[(B) Generator close: the base change of $\rho$ is the inverse regrouping]
2893	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2894	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2895	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2896	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma `lemma-affine-base-change'
2897	  % (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the "boils down to the equality" step
2898	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L933--938).
2899	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the equality
2900	  % $$ (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M $$ as $R'$-modules."
2901	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
2902	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
2903	  Let \(\rho : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), be the
2904	  inner comparison value of Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}. The extension of
2905	  scalars along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of \(\rho\), post-composed with the algebraic counit
2906	  \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) of the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\),
2907	  is an \(R'\)-linear map \(R' \otimes_R M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\); on the generator
2908	  \(r' \otimes m\) it returns \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\),
2909	  which is exactly the value of the inverse regrouping isomorphism
2910	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on \(r' \otimes m\). Both maps being
2911	  \(R'\)-linear and agreeing on the generators \(r' \otimes m\), they coincide:
2912	  \[
2913	    \operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \,\circ\, \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}
2914	      \;=\; \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}.
2915	  \]
2916	\end{lemma}
2917	\begin{proof}
2918	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2919	  Both sides are \(R'\)-linear maps out of \(R' \otimes_R M\), so by extensionality it suffices to
2920	  check the value on the generators \(r' \otimes m\). The left side sends
2921	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \rho(m) = r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) =
2922	  (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), using that \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) reads the extension of
2923	  scalars by acting through the \(R'\)-scalar. The inverse regrouping isomorphism of
2924	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} sends, by definition,
2925	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) (in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation). The two
2926	  values agree on every generator, so the maps are equal. No flatness hypothesis is used.
2927	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
189	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.

72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).

85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special

87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on

101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}

236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}

325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}

425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,

430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,

432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,

435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}
573	
574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
639	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
640	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
641	  Lean.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
662	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
663	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
664	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
665	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
666	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
671	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
673	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
674	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
675	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
676	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
677	  \[
678	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
679	  \]
680	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
681	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
682	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
683	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
684	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
685	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
686	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
687	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
688	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
693	  \leanok
694	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
696	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
697	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
698	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives

2951	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_counit_transport}
2952	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_counit_transport}
2953	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso,
2954	        lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
2955	  Let \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\). Write \(\varepsilon_g\) for the counit of the geometric
2956	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction, \(\Pi_{\psi}\) for the pullback dictionary of
2957	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (reading \(g^*\) of a tilde as extension of scalars along
2958	  \(\psi\), built from \(\gamma_{\psi}\), Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso}), and
2959	  \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) for the counit of the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction
2960	  along \(\psi\). Then the geometric counit, conjugated by \(\Pi_{\psi}\) and the tilde--\(\Gamma\)
2961	  counit on either side, equals the algebraic counit: as a single transport identity,
2962	  \[
2963	    \Pi_{\psi}^{-1} \,\circ\, g^*(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}) \,\circ\, \varepsilon_g
2964	    \;=\;
2965	    \widetilde{(-)}_{R'}(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}) \,\circ\, \varepsilon^{\Gamma},
2966	  \]
2967	  the counit-triangle (zig-zag) coherence for the composed adjunction
2968	  \((\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv \Gamma_{R'}) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\), where \(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}\)
2969	  denotes the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit. This is the counit dual of the unit-across-conjugate
2970	  coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} used in Seam~1.

3055	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read} and the identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
3056	  of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, this transpose is the extension of scalars
3057	  along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading \(\rho\) of the inner
3058	  pushforward comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\), namely the canonical \(R\)-linear map
3059	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) — the affine unit value of
3060	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}; on the generator \(r' \otimes m\) it returns
3061	  \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), which is exactly the inverse of the regrouping isomorphism
3062	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}).
3063	\end{lemma}
3064	\begin{proof}
3065	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand,
3066	        lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_gammaDistribute,
3067	        lem:base_change_mate_unit_value, lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp,
3068	        lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, lem:conjugateEquiv_counit_symm_mathlib,
3069	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id,
3070	        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id}
3071	  This is the closing step of the affine section-level computation; the section
3072	  identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity} follows from it directly.
3073	
3074	  \emph{Counit factorization and the master transport identity.} The base-change map
3075	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose of
3076	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)); by the counit form of the hom-set
3077	  adjunction it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the geometric counit
3078	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining,
3079	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma_{R'}\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma_{R'}(\varepsilon_g)\).
3080	  Instantiating the transposed counit-across-conjugate coherence
3081	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_counit_symm_mathlib} at the two composite adjunctions
3082	  \(\mathrm{adj}_L = (\widetilde{(-)}_R \dashv \Gamma_R) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\) and
3083	  \(\mathrm{adj}_R = (\operatorname{ext}_\psi \dashv \operatorname{restr}_\psi) .
3084	  (\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv \Gamma_{R'})\) with \(\alpha = \beta^{\mathrm{hom}}\) the pushforward
3085	  \(\Gamma\)-comparison, and fusing the two composite-counit splits of \(\mathrm{adj}_L\) and
3086	  \(\mathrm{adj}_R\), yields the \emph{master counit-transport identity}
3087	  \[
3088	    \widetilde{(-)}_{R'}\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\bigr) \circ \varepsilon^{\Gamma}
3089	    \;=\;
3090	    \Pi_{\psi}^{-1} \circ g^*\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}\bigr) \circ \varepsilon_g ,
3091	  \]
3092	  where \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) is the algebraic extension/restriction-of-scalars counit
3093	  along \(\psi\), \(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}\) the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit, and \(\Pi_{\psi}\) the
3094	  pullback dictionary (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}); the dictionary enters because the
3095	  conjugate of \(\beta^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is \(\Pi_{\psi}^{-1}\). The trailing dictionary and
3096	  tilde-counit factors are isomorphisms, so solving this identity for \(\varepsilon_g\) and applying
3097	  \(\Gamma_{R'}\) rewrites \(\Gamma_{R'}(\varepsilon_g)\) in the goal into the algebraic counit
3098	  conjugated by exactly the dictionaries that \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)
3099	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
3100	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) bake in. The remaining \(\sim\)150 lines of
3101	  telescoping are three steps.
3102	
3103	  \emph{(a) Inner reindex} (\(\Gamma_R(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}) = \rho\)). The \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)
3104	  section reading of the inner comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the canonical \(R\)-linear map
3105	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) (the affine inner value). This is reproven
3106	  \emph{inline}, not cited from the leg-reindex wrapper: expand the buried \((g')\)-unit by the
3107	  leg-unit expansion Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand} (whose engine is
3108	  the proved unit-distribution Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}), distribute the
3109	  resulting four factors through \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\) and \(\Gamma\) by
3110	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_gammaDistribute}, collapse the
3111	  pushforward-composition coherences to the identity \(\Gamma\)-image
3112	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id},
3113	  \ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id}), and evaluate the lone surviving affine unit by
3114	  Seam~1 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}); the result is \(\rho\). With the inner value
3115	  available the residual \(g^*\)-leg, conjugated by \(\Pi_{\psi}\), is
3116	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho)\).
3117	
3118	  \emph{(b) One-generator close.} The composite
3119	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) is an \(R'\)-linear map
3120	  \(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\); by \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\)
3121	  extensionality it suffices to evaluate it on the generator \(r' \otimes m\), where it returns
3122	  \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This is exactly the value of the
3123	  inverse regrouping isomorphism \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\) on \(r' \otimes m\), so a
3124	  one-generator \(\operatorname{ext}\) against Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} gives
3125	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}} = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).
3126	
3127	  \emph{(c) Dictionary cancellation.} It remains to match the \(\Pi_{\psi}\) (pullback dictionary,
3128	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and tilde-counit factors appearing in the master
3129	  transport identity against the \(\operatorname{pushforward}\)- and \(\operatorname{pullback}\)-tilde
3130	  dictionaries baked into the domain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) and codomain read
3131	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). These factors are mutually inverse isomorphisms, and cancelling them
3132	  reduces the conjugated geometric transpose to the algebraic composite of step~(b), namely
3133	  \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\). This is the claimed equation
3134	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \ggg \Gamma(\theta) \ggg \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3135	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).
3136	\end{proof}

3338	        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
3339	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,

3343	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
3344	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
3345	
3346	  The proof is a direct module computation: we unfold the \emph{definition} of
3347	  \(\alpha = \mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) and evaluate each adjunction
3348	  unit/counit through the single-step tilde dictionaries, never invoking the
3349	  composite-mate recognition. By
3350	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
3351	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunct of the map
3352	  \[
3353	    \beta : f_*\widetilde M \longrightarrow g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3354	          = f_*\,(g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3355	  \]
3356	  obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
3357	  \(\eta : \widetilde M \to (g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) of the
3358	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the square's commutativity
3359	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Explicitly, as adjunct,
3360	  \(\alpha = \varepsilon_{f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M} \circ g^*(\beta)\), where
3361	  \(\varepsilon\) is the counit of \(g^* \dashv g_*\). We trace each factor on global
3362	  sections over the affine model, on a generator \(r' \otimes m \in R' \otimes_R M\).
3363	
3364	  \emph{Step 1 — the inner unit \(\eta\) is the extension-of-scalars unit.}
3365	  On \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the projection \(g'\) is the
3366	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the algebra inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\)
3367	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}). By the pullback dictionary
3368	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, \((g')^*\widetilde M\) is the tilde of the
3369	  extension of scalars \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and that lemma identifies the
3370	  adjunction unit with the algebraic unit of extension of scalars; hence on sections
3371	  \(\eta\) is the \(A\)-linear map
3372	  \[
3373	    M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M, \qquad
3374	    m \longmapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m .
3375	  \]
3376	  This is forced by the universal property of the tensor product and the algebra map
3377	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\); no mate identity is used.
3378	
3379	  \emph{Step 2 — apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\).} The pushforward
3380	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} reads \(f_*\) of a tilde as
3381	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Restriction of scalars does not move
3382	  elements, so on sections over \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) the map
3383	  \(\beta = f_*(\eta)\) (with codomain rewritten by \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\)) is the
3384	  same assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), now read \(R\)-linearly into
3385	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
3386	
3387	  \emph{Step 3 — apply \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\).} The pullback dictionary
3388	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} for \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\) reads
3389	  \(g^*\) of a tilde as extension of scalars \(R' \otimes_R (-)\) along \(\psi\), and is
3390	  functorial: \(g^*(\beta)\) on sections over \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) is
3391	  \(\operatorname{id}_{R'} \otimes \beta\),
3392	  \[
3393	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow R' \otimes_R \bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr),
3394	    \qquad
3395	    r' \otimes m \longmapsto r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr).
3396	  \]
3397	  The domain \(R' \otimes_R M\) here is exactly \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)'s reading of
3398	  \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde M))\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}).
3399

3401	  Writing \(W = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) for the target \(R'\)-module, the counit
3402	  \(\varepsilon : g^* g_* \widetilde W \to \widetilde W\) of \(g^* \dashv g_*\) is, on
3403	  sections over \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and again by
3404	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (which identifies the counit with the
3405	  algebraic action map of extension of scalars), the \(R'\)-linear map
3406	  \[
3407	    R' \otimes_R \bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\psi W\bigr) \longrightarrow W,
3408	    \qquad r' \otimes w \longmapsto r' \cdot w ,
3409	  \]
3410	  where \(r' \cdot w\) is the \(R'\)-action on \(W\). The codomain \(W\) is exactly
3411	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)'s reading of \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M)\)
3412	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}).

3415	  \[
3416	    r' \otimes m
3417	    \;\overset{g^*(\beta)}{\longmapsto}\;
3418	    r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3419	    \;\overset{\varepsilon}{\longmapsto}\;
3420	    r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3421	    = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m ,
3422	  \]

3425	  = ((r' \cdot 1) \otimes 1) \otimes m = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). Thus
3426	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \ggg \Gamma(\alpha) \ggg \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) is the
3427	  \(R'\)-linear map \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), which is
3428	  precisely \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of
3429	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
3430	  (\(\mathtt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)). No flatness hypothesis is used, and the
3431	  value falls out of the tensor-product universal property together with the algebra
3432	  map \(\varphi : R \to A\) — not from any conjugate/mate coherence identity.

3620	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
3621	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an

3623	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the

3626	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to

3638	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
